description: Labeled anomaly-description -> MITRE ATT&CK technique dataset for evaluating attack attribution (RAG vs zero-shot). Each item lists acceptable top-level technique IDs (a description can legitimately map to more than one). Sub-technique IDs are intentionally rolled up to their parent technique, matching the top-level-technique knowledge base OmniShield embeds.

examples.anomaly_description: Workstation transferred 4.2GB of encrypted files to an external IP at 2AM over an established outbound TCP channel after running a PowerShell script.
A user received an email with a malicious attachment impersonating the IT department asking them to enable macros.
Thousands of rapid failed login attempts against the VPN gateway from a single external host within one minute.
Sequential connection attempts to 5000 TCP ports on a single internal server, characteristic of a port scan.
PowerShell spawned by a Word document executed a base64-encoded command that downloaded a payload.
A dormant service account suddenly authenticated from a new geography and accessed several database servers.
Massive volume of spoofed UDP traffic flooded the web server, saturating its bandwidth and causing an outage.
An attacker used stolen RDP credentials to open a remote desktop session to a finance workstation.
Beaconing HTTPS traffic to a suspicious domain at fixed 60-second intervals indicative of command-and-control.
A scheduled task was created to run a suspicious binary every time the system boots to maintain persistence.
Credential dumping tool accessed LSASS memory to extract plaintext passwords and hashes.
Malware injected code into a legitimate running process to evade detection.
Adversary encrypted files across multiple file shares and dropped a ransom note demanding payment.
Network reconnaissance enumerating internal hosts and open SMB shares across the subnet.
An attacker modified the registry Run key to launch malware automatically at user logon.
Large database export followed by upload to a personal cloud storage service over HTTPS.
Suspicious use of Windows Management Instrumentation to execute commands on remote hosts.
An adversary cleared Windows event logs to remove evidence of their activity.
Phishing email contained a link to a fake Microsoft 365 login page harvesting credentials.
A newly created local administrator account was added to the Domain Admins group.
An unpatched internet-facing web server was compromised through a vulnerability in its application to gain an initial foothold.
An adversary authenticated to internet-exposed corporate remote services from an unmanaged external device to enter the network.
A compromised host used certutil to download additional attacker tooling from an external server.
The dropped binary was heavily packed and its strings were base64 and XOR obfuscated to defeat static analysis.
Outbound traffic was relayed through a chain of intermediary proxy nodes to conceal the true command-and-control destination.
Sensitive documents were compressed into a password-protected RAR archive shortly before an outbound transfer.
A process systematically read and collected documents and spreadsheets from the user's local drive.
Malware periodically captured screenshots of the victim's desktop and saved them locally.
A keylogger hooked the keyboard to record every keystroke, capturing credentials as the user typed.
The attacker stopped the antivirus and database services on the host to prepare it for ransomware deployment.
A malicious DLL was executed via rundll32.exe to run code under a trusted signed Windows binary.
An adversary exploited a vulnerable kernel driver to escalate privileges from a standard user to SYSTEM.
The attacker exploited an SMB service vulnerability on a peer host to move laterally without credentials.
Before encrypting data, the ransomware deleted volume shadow copies to prevent system recovery.
Files from several servers were copied into a single staging directory prior to bulk exfiltration.
Enumeration of all domain user accounts was performed using net user /domain from a compromised host.
Commands were run to gather the host's OS version, hostname, domain, and hardware details.
An adversary accessed and exported the full mailbox contents of a company executive.
AnyDesk remote access software was silently installed to give the attacker persistent interactive control.
Stolen data was tunneled out over DNS queries to bypass egress filtering and web proxies.
